title: Vegetable Bins
summary: A divided open bin with a slanted front lip — for onions and potatoes, or toys. Built from 1x4s planked into panels, with a trick for keeping the panels flat.
description:
This is a simple two-compartment open bin with a low, angled front lip that keeps the contents from spilling out while leaving them easy to grab. It's pitched as a vegetable bin (onions, potatoes, garlic) but works just as well as a toy bin — the source builder notes exactly that.

The interesting technique is the planked panels. Rather than using wide 1x12 boards, each panel — two sides, two dividers, a top and a bottom — is glued up from 1x4s for a planked look. The key trick: alternate the grain direction of adjacent boards (look at the end grain, alternate cup-up and cup-down) so the panels resist warping to one side. If you'd rather skip the glue-up, the plan says you can just build the box from 1x12 boards instead — same result, less work.

The box itself takes almost no load — the bottom sits on the floor, the top rests on the sides — so it's nailed together with glue, no pocket holes needed. The front lip is a shaped 1x4: cut the diagonal sides, then attach a straight 1x4 lip across them. If you want a steeper lip for smaller items (Legos, Lincoln Logs), the plan's tip is to use a 1x6 for the lip instead of a 1x4 and trace its edge onto the lip sides. Paint it and it's done.

tools.note: For gluing up the planked panels; skippable if you build from 1x12.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: All panels and the lip — or substitute 1x12 for the panels.

cutList.note: Glue up into a top and a bottom panel.
Glue up into two side panels.
Glue up into two divider panels.
Cut the diagonal — mark 10" and 2", draw the diagonal, and cut.

steps.title: Build the planked panels
steps.body: Join 1x4 boards with the pocket-hole jig to make the panels: two sides, two dividers, and a top and bottom. As you join them, alternate the grain direction of adjacent boards — check the end grain and set them cup-up, cup-down, cup-up — so the panels resist warping to one side. You can skip this entirely by building the box from 1x12 boards instead.

steps.title: Assemble the box
steps.body: Nail the box together with glue and 2" nails, leaving 10" between each side and the adjacent divider. Pocket holes aren't needed here — the bottom sits on the floor and the top rests on the sides, so there's minimal load on the joints.

steps.title: Add the back
steps.body: Check the box for square, then attach the back with 1-1/4" nails and glue. Nail into the dividers as well as the sides so the whole box is tied together and can't rack.

steps.title: Cut the front lip sides
steps.body: Cut the two lip sides from 1x4: mark 10" up one edge and 2" up the other, draw the diagonal between them, clamp the boards down, and cut along the line. This gives the angled profile that holds contents in while keeping them reachable.

steps.title: Attach the lip and finish
steps.body:
Attach the lip sides, then run the straight 1x4 lip across their front with glue, drilling a few pocket holes from underneath to fasten the bottom edge. For smaller items like Legos or Lincoln Logs, make the lip steeper by using a 1x6 instead and tracing its edge onto the lip sides.

Fill nail holes, sand, and paint.

images.alt: Divided wooden storage bin with an angled front lip
